blocks.body: Had better gives strong advice or a warning: something should be done, or there may be a problem.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"We use had better for strong advice or a warning. It often suggests that there is a risk or an unpleasant result if the person does not act.","rules":[{"label":"STRONG ADVICE","color":"emerald","text":"After had better, use the base verb without to.","example":"You had better leave now."},{"label":"WARNING","color":"blue","text":"The construction often carries an implied consequence: otherwise there will be a problem.","example":"We'd better hurry, or we'll miss the train."},{"label":"NEGATIVE FORM","color":"amber","text":"The negative form is had better not + base verb.","example":"You'd better not touch that wire."}]}

{"title":"1. Form","intro":"The pattern is short but fixed:","items":[{"label":"Affirmative","title":"had better + base verb","subtitle":"You had better call him now."},{"label":"Negative","title":"had better not + base verb","subtitle":"You had better not be late."},{"label":"Contraction","title":"'d better","subtitle":"We'd better go."},{"label":"Question","title":"Hadn't we better ...?","subtitle":"Hadn't we better check first?"}]}

{"title":"2. Main uses","sections":[{"label":"Strong advice","color":"emerald","description":"The speaker sees the action as the best choice in a specific situation.","examples":[{"en":"You'd better see a doctor about that cough.","ua":"You'd better see a doctor about that cough."},{"en":"I'd better finish this report tonight.","ua":"I'd better finish this report tonight."}]},{"label":"Warning about a consequence","color":"blue","description":"There is often a feeling that something unpleasant will happen otherwise.","examples":[{"en":"You'd better back up your files.","ua":"You'd better back up your files."},{"en":"We'd better leave now, or the roads will be blocked.","ua":"We'd better leave now, or the roads will be blocked."}]},{"label":"Not for neutral preference","color":"amber","description":"Had better does not simply mean taste or preference. For that, you often need would rather or sometimes should.","examples":[{"en":"I'd rather stay home tonight.","ua":"I'd rather stay home tonight."},{"en":"You should try the new restaurant.","ua":"You should try the new restaurant."}]}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Adding to after had better.","wrong":"You had better to go now.","right":"You had better go now.","hint":"After had better, use the base verb without to."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Building the negative with do.","wrong":"You don't had better wait.","right":"You had better not wait.","hint":"The negative form is had better not."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Confusing strong advice with simple preference.","wrong":"I'd better have tea than coffee.","right":"I'd rather have tea than coffee.","hint":"For choosing between options, you usually need would rather, not had better."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick rule","items":["Had better + base verb = strong advice or warning.","Negative form: had better not + base verb.","Do not use to after had better.","For simple preference, you usually need would rather, not had better."]}
